comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a browser-based 3D three-body problem simulator, with users sharing feedback on UI issues, solver accuracy, and related projects. Some comments highlight technical details about numerical methods used in simulations.

comments.author: hazrmard
comments.text: Very cool! Interesting how the choice of solver affects the solution. Euler doesn't handle misbehaved equations very well. You can see this in the Helix setup where the bodies just fly off.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies the limitations of the Euler method in numerical simulations, providing a relevant example that aligns with known issues in solving differential equations.

comments.author: westurner
comments.text: Notes re: Multi-body problems on "Show HN: Browser-based interactive 3D Three-Body problem simulator" (2025) https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45981336 which has Velocity Verlet integration method, 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4), and Kepler solvers
comments.analysis: This comment adds valuable context by referencing advanced numerical methods commonly used in physics simulations, enhancing the technical depth of the discussion.

comments.author: neondude
comments.text: Feedback : The ui covers more than half my screen on mobile browser
comments.analysis: A straightforward usability critique that highlights a practical issue with the UI on mobile devices, which is important for user experience.

comments.author: ubezon
comments.text: Very cool! nice to be able to export json too
comments.analysis: Expresses positive feedback and notes a useful feature, contributing to the overall appreciation of the project.

comments.author: hermitcrab
comments.text: Random3D seems to always have all the stars heading off to infinity.
comments.analysis: Observes a specific behavior in the simulation that may indicate instability or numerical issues, which is a relevant technical insight.

comments.author: curseofcasandra
comments.text: The tri-Solarians could have just used this!
comments.analysis: A humorous, fictional reference that adds levity but does not contribute substantive technical content.

comments.author: kordlessagain
comments.analysis: A brief, unclear comment lacking context or explanation, making it unhelpful for the discussion.
